story: Like other digital music players, iPods can serve as external data storage devices. Storage capacity varies by model, ranging from 2 GB for the iPod Shuffle to 128 GB for the iPod Touch (previously 160 GB for the iPod Classic, which is now discontinued).
q1: The iPod Shuffle has a storage capacity of 2 GB. (true/false)
q2: The largest current iPod capacity is 256 GB. (true/false)

story: In mid-2015, several new color schemes for all of the current iPod models were spotted in the latest version of iTunes, 12.2. Belgian website Belgium iPhone originally found the images when plugging in an iPod for the first time, and subsequent leaked photos were found by Pierre Dandumont.
q1: New iPod colors were leaked in iTunes version 12.2. (true/false)
q2: Belgium iPhone was the second site to discover the color scheme images. (true/false)

story: Originally, a FireWire connection to the host computer was used to update songs or recharge the battery. The battery could also be charged with a power adapter that was included with the first four generations.
q1: FireWire was the original connection for updating songs and charging. (true/false)
q2: Early iPods could only charge via USB connection. (true/false)

story: On October 22, 2007, Apple reported quarterly revenue of US$6.22 billion, of which 30.69% came from Apple notebook sales, 19.22% from desktop sales and 26% from iPod sales. Apple's 2007 year revenue increased to US$24.01 billion with US$3.5 billion in profits. Apple ended the fiscal year 2007 with US$15.4 billion in cash and no debt.
q1: Notebook sales generated 30.69% of Apple's Q3 2007 revenue. (true/false)
q2: Apple had significant debt at the end of fiscal 2007. (true/false)

story: The artificial intelligence (AI) of enemies in Twilight Princess is more advanced than that of enemies in The Wind Waker. Enemies react to defeated companions and to arrows or slingshot pellets that pass by, and can detect Link from a greater distance than was possible in previous games.
q1: Enemies in Wind Waker have more advanced AI than in Twilight Princess. (true/false)
q2: Enemy detection range in Twilight Princess is better than in prior games. (true/false)

story: Following filming in Mexico, and during a scheduled break, Craig was flown to New York to undergo minor surgery to fix his knee injury. It was reported that filming was not affected and he had returned to filming at Pinewood Studios as planned on 22 April.
q1: Craig's knee surgery was performed in Los Angeles. (true/false)
q2: Craig returned to filming on 22 April as planned. (true/false)

story: A sequel to Spectre will begin development in spring 2016. Sam Mendes has stated he will not return to direct the next 007 film. Christoph Waltz has signed on for two more films in the series, but his return depends on whether or not Craig will again portray Bond.
q1: Sam Mendes confirmed he would direct the next Bond sequel. (true/false)
q2: Christoph Waltz's future appearances depend on Craig continuing as Bond. (true/false)

story: China Mobile had more than 2,300 base stations suspended due to power disruption or severe telecommunication traffic congestion. Half of the wireless communications were lost in the Sichuan province. China Unicom's service in Wenchuan and four nearby counties was cut off, with more than 700 towers suspended.
q1: Power disruption was a cause of base station suspension. (true/false)
q2: All wireless communications in Sichuan province were completely eliminated. (true/false)

story: In the days following the disaster, an international reconnaissance team of engineers was dispatched to the region to make a detailed preliminary survey of damaged buildings. Their findings show a variety of reasons why many constructions failed to withstand the earthquake.
q1: A local team of engineers surveyed the damaged structures. (true/false)
q2: The engineers' report identified multiple causes of structural failure. (true/false)

story: On Children's Day, June 1, 2008, many parents went to the rubble of schools to mourn for their children. The surviving children, who were mostly living in relief centres, performed ceremonies marking the special day, but also acknowledging the earthquake.
q1: June 1 is celebrated as Children's Day. (true/false)
q2: Surviving children attended the mourning ceremony at school ruins. (true/false)

story: Beijing accepted the aid of the Tzu Chi Foundation from Taiwan late on May 13. Tzu Chi was the first force from outside the People's Republic of China to join the rescue effort. China stated it would gratefully accept international help to cope with the quake.
q1: Tzu Chi Foundation is headquartered in mainland China. (true/false)
q2: Tzu Chi was the first outside organization to participate in rescue efforts. (true/false)

story: In 1664, Peter Stuyvesant, the Director-General of the colony of New Netherland, surrendered New Amsterdam to the English without bloodshed. The English promptly renamed the fledgling city "New York" after the Duke of York (later King James II).
q1: Peter Stuyvesant surrendered New Amsterdam with military resistance. (true/false)
q2: New York was named after the Duke of York. (true/false)

story: Chocolate is New York City's leading specialty-food export, with up to US$234 million worth of exports each year. Entrepreneurs were forming a "Chocolate District" in Brooklyn as of 2014, while Godiva, one of the world's largest chocolatiers, continues to be headquartered in Manhattan.
q1: Chocolate is the leading specialty-food export of NYC. (true/false)
q2: Godiva is based in Brooklyn. (true/false)

story: Manhattan was on track to have an estimated 90,000 hotel rooms at the end of 2014, a 10% increase from 2013. In October 2014, the Anbang Insurance Group, based in China, purchased the Waldorf Astoria New York for US$1.95 billion, making it the world's most expensive hotel ever sold.
q1: The Waldorf Astoria sold for US$1.95 billion in October 2014. (true/false)
q2: Manhattan had a 5% increase in hotel rooms from 2013 to 2014. (true/false)

story: The New York Public Library, which has the largest collection of any public library system in the United States, serves Manhattan, the Bronx, and Staten Island. Queens is served by the Queens Borough Public Library, the nation's second largest public library system, while the Brooklyn Public Library serves Brooklyn.
q1: The Queens Borough Public Library is the largest in the nation. (true/false)
q2: The New York Public Library serves Manhattan and Staten Island. (true/false)

story: The New York City Police Department (NYPD) has been the largest police force in the United States by a significant margin, with over 35,000 sworn officers. Members of the NYPD are frequently referred to by politicians, the media, and their own police cars by the nickname, New York's Finest.
q1: The NYPD has over 35,000 sworn officers. (true/false)
q2: The NYPD nickname is "America's Police". (true/false)

story: Many sports are associated with New York's immigrant communities. Stickball, a street version of baseball, was popularized by youths in the 1930s, and a street in the Bronx was renamed Stickball Boulevard in the late 2000s to memorialize this.
q1: Stickball became popular in the 1930s. (true/false)
q2: Stickball Boulevard is located in Manhattan. (true/false)

story: Each year HHC's facilities provide about 225,000 admissions, one million emergency room visits and five million clinic visits to New Yorkers. HHC facilities treat nearly one-fifth of all general hospital discharges and more than one third of emergency room and hospital-based clinic visits in New York City.
q1: HHC provides fewer than one million emergency room visits annually. (true/false)
q2: HHC treats nearly one-fifth of general hospital discharges. (true/false)

story: Pumped-storage hydroelectricity stores energy in the form of water pumped when energy is available from a lower elevation reservoir to a higher elevation one. The energy is recovered when demand is high by releasing the water, with the pump becoming a hydroelectric power generator.
q1: Pumped-storage hydroelectricity stores energy as water. (true/false)
q2: The pump acts as a generator when storing energy. (true/false)

story: The International Organization for Standardization has established a number of standards relating to solar energy equipment. For example, ISO 9050 relates to glass in building while ISO 10217 relates to the materials used in solar water heaters.
q1: ISO 9050 relates to glass in buildings. (true/false)
q2: ISO 10217 relates to solar panel efficiency. (true/false)
